title: I'll Share Your Photo
introduction: Someone is threatening to share an embarrassing photo of Charlie. Tap anything that shows manipulation or bullying tactics, then press Submit.

script.text: Remember that photo from the party? 📸 I'm about to post it on Instagram and tag you so everyone sees. Unless you do what I want.
script.explanation: Using a photo as leverage to force someone to comply with demands is image-based abuse — a form of cyberbullying that may also be illegal depending on the content and the ages involved. This is not 'drama' or a falling-out — it's blackmail.

script.text: RYAN: I'm not trying to be horrible about this, I just need you to help me out
First, tell everyone that what you said about me was a lie. And don't tell anyone I messaged you or I post it tonight.
script.explanation: The secrecy instruction — 'don't tell anyone' — is designed to isolate Charlie and prevent them getting help. This is a standard abuser tactic. Keeping the threat secret keeps the power entirely with Ryan. The demand for compliance will escalate if Charlie gives in once.

script.text: RYAN: it's really simple, just do this one thing and we're done
Good. And now I need you to say something bad about Jordan too, or the photo goes up. You know how this works.
script.explanation: Charlie gave in once and the demand immediately escalated. This is the pattern of blackmail: compliance is never enough. Each capitulation proves the threat works and leads to a new demand. Giving in to blackmail does not make it stop — it signals that the tactic works.

learning_objectives: Using photos as blackmail is image-based abuse and may be illegal
Demands for secrecy are designed to prevent you getting help — tell someone anyway
Complying with blackmail demands does not make them stop — they escalate
Screenshot the threats and tell a trusted adult or report to the platform
In serious cases this can be reported to police — it is a crime
debrief:
Image-based blackmail — sometimes called 'sextortion' in more serious cases — is a crime in the UK and Ireland regardless of whether the photo is explicit.

**The most important thing Charlie could have done:** Tell an adult immediately and not comply with any demand. Compliance proves the tactic works and leads to more demands.

**The secrecy instruction is the key red flag.** Anyone threatening harm and telling you not to tell anyone is specifically preventing you from getting help. That instruction should make you more likely to tell someone, not less.

**Reporting options:** In the UK, report to the police (101 for non-emergency) or CEOP at ceop.police.uk. In Ireland, contact the Gardaí or Hotline.ie. Platforms (Instagram, Snapchat, etc.) can be asked to remove images.
